content.assessment: Delaying selection age directly targets the centralized entrance-exam mechanism operating at age 10 (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03), which underlies Hungary's unusually high share of selective schools (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01). This is a high system-coverage intervention in principle, since it changes the core institutional sorting point rather than a peripheral lever. However, institutional fit is questionable: church-maintained and private schools retain admissions exemptions (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05) and are not clearly brought within a delayed-selection regime, and geographic sorting into vocational tracks (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06) operates independently of the academic selection age being targeted, meaning full national coverage of stratification drivers would not be achieved even if the reform succeeded as designed. The contested tracking-effects evidence (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04) further complicates institutional assessment of what would replace the abolished selection point.
opportunities: Could reduce the scope of the pre-adolescent sorting mechanism most directly linked to Hungary's outlier selection statistics
strengths: Targets the core centralized admissions mechanism identified as producing outlier selection rates relative to OECD peers
Addresses a documented age-10 sorting point rather than a downstream symptom
threats: Church/private school admissions exemptions remain a parallel unregulated channel that could absorb selection demand and undermine coverage of any age-based reform
weaknesses: Does not by design address maintainer-exemption sorting or geographic/settlement-driven stratification, leaving substantial segregation channels untouched
Contested evidence on tracking's differential effects on remaining non-tracked-school students complicates predicting the net institutional outcome
